regulator: MAS (Monetary Authority of Singapore) -- sole regulator; no separate funds/ETF or securities regulator exists in Singapore
instruments: MAS media release, 'MAS Expands Industry Collaboration to Scale Asset Tokenisation for Financial Services', Singapore, 27 June 2024 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF via the Singapore Government's official press-release repository (sgpc.gov.sg), since every path tried on mas.gov.sg itself, including this release's own URL, returns an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' HTML shell; states that MAS, over the preceding two years, had worked with 24 financial institutions to pilot asset-tokenisation use cases under Project Guardian, and that a new 'Asset & Wealth Management workstream will deepen collaboration with global custodians and asset managers, focus on common data models, and model risk considerations specific to fund tokenisation'; Annex A of this release lists, among the 24 participating institutions and their MAS-stated use cases, several described in MAS's own words as engaged in 'Digital issuance of VCC funds' (Franklin Templeton; Schroder Investment Management Limited) or 'Digital issuance of VCC funds (asset servicing)' (Hongkong and Shanghai Banking Corporation; J.P. Morgan; SBI Digital Markets; Standard Chartered) or 'Scalable portfolio management including VCCs' (Apollo; J.P. Morgan) or 'Aggregation of investment servicing activities for asset and wealth managers launching tokenised/digital funds' (Deutsche Bank)

MAS media release, 'MAS Announces Plans to Support Commercialisation of Asset Tokenisation', Singapore, 4 November 2024 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the genuine PDF via sgpc.gov.sg (mas.gov.sg itself blocked, same Maintenance shell); states in MAS's own words that 'two industry frameworks developed by Project Guardian industry group members were published today', and describes the Guardian Funds Framework (GFF) verbatim as follows: 'GFF provides a set of recommendations for industry best practices for tokenised funds. This includes the Guardian Composable Token Taxonomy to facilitate development of tokenised investment vehicles comprising multiple assets, simplifying the process of incorporating new tokenised funds, and help achieve efficiencies in fund settlement'; the same release records that Citi, HSBC, Schroders, Standard Chartered and UOB 'have formed the Guardian Wholesale Network industry group' to commercialise asset-tokenisation trials (not fund-specific), and that Project Guardian by this date spanned 'over 40 financial institutions, industry associations and international policymakers across seven jurisdictions' with 'more than 15 industry trials ... conducted in six currencies across multiple financial products' including funds; this release's updated Annex A additionally lists Citi and Fidelity as engaged in a 'Digital Money Market Fund and FX Swap solution' use case

Leong Sing Chiong (Deputy Managing Director, Markets & Development, MAS), opening keynote address 'Tokenisation in Financial Services: Pathways to Scale', Layer One Summit, Singapore, 4 November 2024 -- confirmed by direct fetch of the Bank for International Settlements' official reproduction of the speech (bis.org/review/r241104k.htm), used here only because mas.gov.sg's own speeches page returns the same Maintenance block described above; MAS's own Deputy Managing Director states that the Guardian Funds Framework was published 'today' with the content described above, cites the Citi/Fidelity International tokenised Money Market Fund plus FX-swap solution by name as a live example, and states candidly that despite numerous industry pilots across Project Guardian generally 'no one has really succeeded in achieving scale' -- a general tokenisation-scaling observation, not specific to funds alone

MAS: Project Guardian programme page (mas.gov.sg/schemes-and-initiatives/project-guardian) and the Guardian Funds Framework and 'Operationalising Tokenised Funds' documents themselves (mas.gov.sg/-/media/mas-media-library/development/fintech/guardian/) -- link-listed only; every path tried against mas.gov.sg this session, including these documents' own direct PDF URLs, returned an identical 853,817-byte HTML page titled 'Maintenance' with noindex,nofollow, to this project's honest User-Agent and to plain curl alike, verified 2026-07-13; the underlying documents' full text could not be independently fetched or quoted this session -- see open_items

Securities and Futures Act 2001 (SFA) and the MAS Code on Collective Investment Schemes -- the general, technology-neutral statutory and regulatory basis on which authorised/recognised collective investment schemes (including exchange-traded funds) are offered to investors in Singapore; their existence and MAS's administration of them is well established from this jurisdiction's other pillar cards (e.g. the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar's direct fetch of MAS's own 6 June 2025 media release confirming SFA/FAA applicability to capital-markets-product tokens), but this research pass could not itself fetch or quote either the SFA's or the CIS Code's operative text: Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg) returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried, including the SFA's own Act page and robots.txt, and the CIS Code's own PDF path on mas.gov.sg returned the same Maintenance block described above -- both re-confirmed live in this research session, 2026-07-13; see open_items

Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg) -- registered for link-listing only. Live-verified in this research session: every path tried, including the Securities and Futures Act's own Act page and robots.txt, returns an identical HTTP 403 CloudFront error ('ERROR: The request could not be satisfied ... Request blocked'), so no statutory text bearing on collective investment schemes or ETFs could be directly fetched or quoted this session
standing_summary: Singapore has no dedicated licensing or authorisation regime for crypto-asset funds or crypto-asset exchange-traded funds, and no crypto-specific statute governing this pillar: funds and ETFs, tokenised or not, fall under the Monetary Authority of Singapore's general, technology-neutral framework for collective investment schemes under the Securities and Futures Act 2001 and the MAS Code on Collective Investment Schemes -- the same regime tracked from the capital-markets-product angle in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar. This research pass could not itself fetch or quote either instrument's operative text: Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg) returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried this session, including the SFA's own Act page and robots.txt, and the CIS Code's PDF path on mas.gov.sg returned the same 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' HTML shell that blocks every other path tried on that domain -- both re-confirmed live today, 2026-07-13. What this research did directly confirm, via genuine PDF fetches routed through the Singapore Government's official press-release repository (sgpc.gov.sg) rather than mas.gov.sg itself, is that MAS's substantive activity specific to this pillar today is confined to Project Guardian, a MAS-convened, cross-industry collaboration on asset tokenisation -- not a licensing or authorisation regime. MAS's own 27 June 2024 media release records that, over the two years to that date, MAS had worked with 24 financial institutions piloting tokenisation use cases under Project Guardian, and announces the formation of an 'Asset & Wealth Management workstream' that would 'deepen collaboration with global custodians and asset managers, focus on common data models, and model risk considerations specific to fund tokenisation'; that release's own Annex A lists several of those 24 institutions' MAS-stated use cases as involving digital issuance of Variable Capital Company (VCC) funds or VCC-based portfolio management -- Singapore's general corporate fund vehicle, administered on the company-law side by the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority (ACRA), used here in a tokenisation pilot context rather than as evidence of any independent ACRA digital-asset regulatory role. The concrete deliverable for this pillar arrived four months later: MAS's own 4 November 2024 media release states, in MAS's own words, that two industry frameworks developed by Project Guardian industry-group members were published that day, and describes the Guardian Funds Framework (GFF) as follows -- 'GFF provides a set of recommendations for industry best practices for tokenised funds. This includes the Guardian Composable Token Taxonomy to facilitate development of tokenised investment vehicles comprising multiple assets, simplifying the process of incorporating new tokenised funds, and help achieve efficiencies in fund settlement.' The framing is explicit and load-bearing for this card's status_seal: the GFF is industry-authored recommendations and best practices, published under MAS's convening umbrella, not a MAS rule, notice, or licensing condition, and this card does not treat it as one. That same release's updated Annex A adds Citi and Fidelity International as engaged in a 'Digital Money Market Fund and FX Swap solution' use case, and records that Citi, HSBC, Schroders, Standard Chartered and UOB had by then formed the 'Guardian Wholesale Network' industry group to commercialise tokenisation trials generally (spanning multiple asset classes, not funds alone); by that date Project Guardian counted 'over 40 financial institutions, industry associations and international policymakers across seven jurisdictions', with 'more than 15 industry trials ... conducted in six currencies across multiple financial products' including funds. A speech by MAS's own Deputy Managing Director (Markets & Development), Leong Sing Chiong, given the same day at the Layer One Summit -- fetched via the Bank for International Settlements' official reproduction (bis.org) because mas.gov.sg's own speeches section is identically blocked -- confirms the GFF's same-day publication, names the Citi/Fidelity tokenised money-market-fund-plus-FX-swap solution as a live example, and states candidly that despite numerous pilots across Project Guardian as a whole, 'no one has really succeeded in achieving scale' -- a general observation about tokenisation, not specific to funds. This research also located, but could not independently fetch or verify against any primary MAS document this session, secondary reporting of two further developments potentially material to this pillar: (i) that MAS launched a dedicated 'funds workstream' within Project Guardian as early as its 15 November 2023 media release, focused on native issuance of VCC funds on digital-asset networks and reportedly involving MAS collaboration with ACRA on tax, policy and legal considerations -- no sgpc.gov.sg mirror of that specific release could be located this session and mas.gov.sg itself is blocked, so this is recorded here as an unverified background item only, not as an independently confirmed primary fact, and does not alter this card's finding (consistent with the Stablecoins pillar's own finding) that ACRA's role remains general company registration rather than an independent digital-asset regulatory function; and (ii) that a further Project Guardian document, 'Operationalising Tokenised Funds', described by secondary sources as a detailed operational playbook covering fund structuring models, settlement assets, custody, KYC/AML and cross-chain interoperability for tokenised funds, was published in November 2025 -- secondary sources disagree on the exact date (12 November per one source, 27 November per another), the document's own PDF is hosted only on the blocked mas.gov.sg domain, and no sgpc.gov.sg mirror was located this session, so neither its content nor its precise publication date is asserted here as independently verified. Separately, this research searched specifically for a primary MAS statement on whether spot Bitcoin/crypto exchange-traded funds may be offered to retail investors in Singapore -- widely reported in January 2024 secondary press (originating in Chinese-language reporting by Zaobao and repeated by multiple crypto-trade outlets) as an MAS spokesperson statement that digital payment tokens are not eligible assets for retail collective investment schemes and that spot Bitcoin ETFs are accordingly 'not approved by MAS for offer to retail investors', while noting retail investors may still access overseas-listed spot Bitcoin ETFs through licensed intermediaries subject to suitability checks. This research could not locate that statement on any genuinely fetchable primary channel (mas.gov.sg is blocked; no sgpc.gov.sg mirror of a matching press release or parliamentary reply was found; it reads as a response to a media query rather than a published release) and therefore, per this card's sourcing discipline, does not assert it as fact in this summary -- it is recorded in open_items instead. This pillar covers fund and ETF product-level regulation and the Project Guardian funds/asset-and-wealth-management workstream only; wholesale CBDC and settlement-asset infrastructure (SGD Testnet, BLOOM) are tracked in the Banking & Money pillar; capital-markets-product token dealing and advisory regulation generally (SFA/FAA licensing) is tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar -- neither is duplicated here.

key_links.label: Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Expands Industry Collaboration to Scale Asset Tokenisation for Financial Services' (27 Jun 2024) -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session; source of the Asset & Wealth Management workstream and Annex A VCC-fund use-case quotes above

Singapore Press Centre (sgpc.gov.sg): MAS media release, 'MAS Announces Plans to Support Commercialisation of Asset Tokenisation' (4 Nov 2024) -- official PDF, fetch-verified live this session; source of the Guardian Funds Framework quote above

Bank for International Settlements: official reproduction of Leong Sing Chiong (MAS), 'Tokenisation in Financial Services: Pathways to Scale', Layer One Summit keynote, 4 Nov 2024 -- fetch-verified live this session; used because mas.gov.sg's own speeches page is blocked

MAS: Guardian Funds Framework (PDF) -- link only; every path tried on www.mas.gov.sg, including this document's own PDF path, served an identical 853,817-byte 'Maintenance' bot-block page to this project's honest User-Agent and plain curl alike, verified this session; cannot currently satisfy the quote-authenticity gate

MAS: 'Operationalising Tokenised Funds' (Project Guardian Asset & Wealth Management Industry Group report, reported in secondary sources as published November 2025) -- link only; www.mas.gov.sg blocked on this path this session; publication date and content not independently confirmed, see open_items
MAS: Project Guardian programme page -- link only; www.mas.gov.sg blocked on this path this session

MAS: Code on Collective Investment Schemes (PDF) -- link only; www.mas.gov.sg blocked on this path this session; the general retail-CIS/ETF-eligible-assets framework this card could not independently fetch or quote

Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg): Securities and Futures Act 2001 -- link only; returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried this session, including robots.txt; cannot currently satisfy the quote-authenticity gate

open_items: This research could not locate a genuinely primary, independently fetchable MAS statement addressing whether spot Bitcoin/crypto exchange-traded funds may be offered to retail investors in Singapore. Multiple secondary sources (crypto-trade press, originating in January 2024 Chinese-language reporting by Zaobao) report an MAS spokesperson statement that digital payment tokens are not eligible assets for retail collective investment schemes, and that spot Bitcoin ETFs are accordingly 'not approved by MAS for offer to retail investors', with overseas-listed spot Bitcoin ETFs remaining accessible to retail investors only through licensed intermediaries subject to suitability checks. mas.gov.sg is blocked on every path tried, and no sgpc.gov.sg mirror of a matching press release or parliamentary reply was found; the statement reads as a reply to a media query rather than a published release, which would explain the absence of any repository mirror. Per this card's sourcing discipline, this is recorded here only, not asserted in the standing_summary as fact. Should be re-investigated once mas.gov.sg is fetchable, including a direct search of MAS's own parliamentary-replies listing.

Secondary sources (a CryptoSlate report and an Allen & Gledhill client alert, among others) describe MAS's 15 November 2023 media release, 'MAS Partners Financial Industry to Expand Asset Tokenisation Initiatives', as launching a dedicated Project Guardian 'funds workstream' focused on native issuance of Variable Capital Company (VCC) funds on digital-asset networks, reportedly involving MAS collaboration with the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority (ACRA) on tax, policy and legal considerations, with Franklin Templeton reportedly exploring a tokenised money-market VCC fund as a named pilot. This research could not fetch that release directly: mas.gov.sg is blocked on every path tried, and no sgpc.gov.sg mirror was located despite a specific search. This item is accordingly recorded as unverified background only; it is consistent with, but does not itself confirm, the VCC-fund use cases independently confirmed via the genuinely fetched 27 June 2024 and 4 November 2024 releases cited in this card's instruments list.

'Operationalising Tokenised Funds', a further Project Guardian Asset & Wealth Management Industry Group document covering fund structuring models, settlement assets, custody, KYC/AML and interoperability for tokenised funds, is referenced by its own title on mas.gov.sg's media-library path and described by secondary sources as published in November 2025 -- but those secondary sources disagree on the exact date (12 November per one source, 27 November per another), and this research could not independently fetch the document itself (mas.gov.sg blocked) or locate an sgpc.gov.sg mirror of any announcing press release. Neither the document's content nor its precise publication date is treated as confirmed in this card; both should be independently re-verified once mas.gov.sg is fetchable.

This card could not independently fetch or quote the operative text of either the Securities and Futures Act 2001 or the MAS Code on Collective Investment Schemes -- the general, technology-neutral statutory basis under which authorised/recognised collective investment schemes, including any future tokenised fund or crypto-asset ETF, would be offered in Singapore. Singapore Statutes Online (sso.agc.gov.sg) returned a live-verified HTTP 403 CloudFront block on every path tried this session, including the SFA's own Act page and robots.txt, and the CIS Code's PDF path on mas.gov.sg returned the same 'Maintenance' bot-block described throughout this card. Consequently, this card cannot state from primary text (a) the CIS Code's own eligible-assets criteria for retail schemes, or (b) whether or how those criteria treat digital payment tokens specifically -- both should be independently re-verified once either domain is fetchable.

The exact launch date of Project Guardian itself (widely and consistently reported, including by MAS's own media-release listing snippet captured in this session's search results, as 31 May 2022, at the Asia Tech x Singapore Summit) was not independently confirmed via direct fetch of MAS's own launch release this session: mas.gov.sg is blocked on that path, and no sgpc.gov.sg mirror was located. This card relies instead on Project Guardian's existence and general scope as independently confirmed via the two genuinely fetched 2024 media releases in the instruments list, which both refer to Project Guardian as an established, ongoing initiative as of their own dates.

Boundary note: this pillar covers fund and ETF product-level regulation and the Project Guardian funds/asset-and-wealth-management workstream only. Wholesale CBDC and common settlement-asset infrastructure (SGD Testnet, the BLOOM initiative) are tracked in the Banking & Money pillar; capital-markets-product token dealing and advisory regulation generally, including SFA/FAA licensing applicability to tokenised securities, is tracked in the Dealing, Custody, Advisory & Management pillar; and the Accounting and Corporate Regulatory Authority's general (non-digital-asset-specific) role in Variable Capital Company registration is not restated here beyond what bears directly on the VCC-fund tokenisation use cases cited above -- none of that content is duplicated in this card.
